text: The surgeon begins the operation by probing the external opening of the fistula and tracing the tract to the internal opening. A retractor is placed in the anal canal, and a metal probe is left in the fistula to aid in dissection. An incision is made at the anocutaneous border, revealing the intersphincteric space and the intersphincteric part of the fistula tract.

The surgeon dissects and ligates the fistula tract adjacent to the internal and external sphincters. The tract is cut between the ligatures, and a fistulascope is inserted through the external opening to explore for secondary branches. Fulguration is performed on the fistula, followed by curettage of necrotic tissue.

The surgical wound is irrigated with hydrogen peroxide and saline solution. The space is closed with resorbable interrupted sutures along the incision on the endocutaneous border. An incision is made on the contralateral side of the anacutaneous border, where the internal sphincter is identified. A small sphincterotomy is performed to ease the anal structure, and the wound is closed with resorbable interrupted sutures.
The surgeon excises the external fistula opening to facilitate healing. The surgical site appears clean, with no signs of fecal incontinence observed.
